Kenya.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation in Kenya through policy assessments, financial tracking, and technological innovations. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed Kenya’s climate adaptation and mitigation frameworks for innovation uptake (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181177). 
- CGIAR tracked finance for locally led climate action and identified challenges (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180613).
Kenya.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed Kenya’s climate adaptation and mitigation frameworks for innovation uptake, highlighting gaps in implementation and coordination (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181177).
• CGIAR tracked finance for locally led climate action, identifying challenges in transparency and reporting (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180613).
• CGIAR developed climate-smart financial solutions to enhance smallholder farmers' resilience to climate risks (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169396).
• CGIAR explored the policy landscape for climate action in Kenya, emphasizing the need for better coordination and monitoring (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174475).
• CGIAR used public narrative storytelling to communicate climate resilience actions in Kenya, amplifying Indigenous communities' experiences (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138221).
• CGIAR assessed Kenya’s County Climate Change Fund for conflict-sensitive adaptation governance (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169314).
• CGIAR reviewed technical guidelines for designing, implementing, and tracking climate adaptation policies in Africa (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180194).
• CGIAR evaluated the impact of credit products on food security and resilience in semi-arid regions of Kenya (Source_https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OBZUCX).
Kenya.mitigation: • The Bioversity–CIAT Alliance developed a digitized crop-activity data tool to strengthen Kenyan governmental reporting on crop greenhouse gas emissions (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181368).

Ethiopia.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agricultural development in Ethiopia through targeted research and interventions. Specifically:
Ethiopia.adaptation:
• IFPRI advanced climate-smart financial solutions to enhance smallholder farmers' resilience to climate risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169396).
• IFPRI evaluated the impact of multisectoral interventions on livelihoods, food security, and well-being through the SPIR project (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NYTIMZ).
• IFPRI conducted a randomized controlled trial to promote sustainable land management practices like fruit tree planting and composting (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BELDFL).
• CGIAR stakeholders developed a Climate-Smart Agriculture Investment Plan focusing on resilience, productivity, and adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145352).
• CGIAR integrated environmental and social safeguards into climate-smart agriculture initiatives through training programs (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168776).
• CGIAR provided a quick reference guide on using theories of change for monitoring climate change adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/126808).
• CGIAR stakeholders outlined priority investments for Climate-Smart Agriculture in Ethiopia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128308).
Ethiopia.mitigation: • CGIAR stakeholders identified investments in Climate-Smart Agriculture that address mitigation alongside adaptation and productivity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128308).
Ethiopia.water:
• IFPRI studied the connections between irrigation, gender, nutrition, and health in Ethiopia as part of the CGIAR Research Program on Water, Land and Ecosystems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/AHVXHQ).
• IFPRI conducted surveys to investigate the impact of small-scale irrigation on agricultural production and household well-being (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7E7LG7).
• IFPRI explored the role of irrigation in improving nutrition and health outcomes in Ethiopia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SSFGDG).

Peru.narrative: CGIAR and its centers have contributed to climate change adaptation and mitigation in Peru through various research initiatives. Specifically:
Peru.adaptation:
• The Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT supported implementing Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (LTACs) action plans through capacity-building workshops to enhance climate resilience in agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174584).
• The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security provided a climate hazard-based Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) practice prioritization and cost-benefit analysis for cocoa in Peru (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93343).
• IFPRI conducted a multiyear study to assess the potential impact of climate change on the agriculture sector in Peru through 2050, integrating results from climate and economic models (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146583).
• The Climate Action Platform in Agriculture for Latin America and the Caribbean (PLACA) prioritized LTACs in their 2024 action plan as a strategic mechanism to enhance climate resilience and sustainability in agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174584).
• The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security examined the interconnectedness between climate change and food and nutrition security in Latin America and offered methods for measuring their impacts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948).
• The Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT developed Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA) in agroecological communities of Ecuador, Peru, and Colombia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116798).
Peru.mitigation:
• The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security prioritized Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices for cocoa in Peru, emphasizing the reduction of GHG emissions from cocoa production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93343).
• The World Bank, Centro Agronómico Tropical de Investigación y Enseñanza, and International Center for Tropical Agriculture highlighted the importance of climate-smart agriculture in reducing/removing greenhouse gases (GHGs) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/52993).
• The Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT worked on low-carbon agriculture projects in Peru (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116798).

Mali.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation in Mali through various innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed the Conflict–Climate Pressure Index (CCPI) to enhance anticipatory capacity in food crises early warning systems (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179392).
- CGIAR scaled climate-smart agriculture technologies and innovations through capacity building and practical training workshops (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179609).
- CGIAR promoted the adoption of drought- and flood–tolerant rice varieties and good agricultural practices to increase rice yields and climate resilience (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179427).
- CGIAR provided over 200,000 recommendations to rice farmers using the digital advisory tool RiceAdvice to support climate-smart agriculture practices (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179425).
- CGIAR built capacities of university lecturers in climate-smart agriculture technologies and climate information services (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514).
- CGIAR examined how climate change and conflict disrupt children’s education across the Sahel, focusing on Mali (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179801).
- CGIAR identified specific needs of women, youth, and other vulnerable groups in climate information services to build local resilience (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179513).
- CGIAR supported a regional workshop to build the resource mobilization capacity of civil society organizations for climate finance (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).
- CGIAR reviewed climate-smart livestock interventions in West Africa, focusing on mitigation opportunities (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76294).
- CGIAR worked to achieve climate resilience by strengthening gender equality and social inclusion across food systems (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119245).

- CGIAR assessed climate risk profiles and adaptation options in Visayas, Luzon, and Mindanao (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/113568, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/113512, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/113513).

Niger.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation in Niger through various innovations. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed the Conflict–Climate Pressure Index (CCPI) to enhance anticipatory capacity in food crises early warning systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179392). 
- CGIAR scaled climate-smart agriculture technologies and innovations through capacity building and promotion (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179609). 
- CGIAR assessed adaptation strategies to climate change and their impacts on household income and food security (https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847). 
- CGIAR mainstreamed Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) and Climate Information Service (CIS) into universities’ curricula (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514). 
- CGIAR examined how climate change and conflict disrupt children’s education across the Sahel (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179801). 
- CGIAR strengthened gender and social inclusion in climate information services (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179513). 
- CGIAR conducted a regional needs assessment for mainstreaming climate-smart agriculture and climate information services into universities’ curricula (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179518). 
- CGIAR reviewed projections of demand and supply of livestock-derived foods and the implications for livestock sector management (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101135). 
- CGIAR planned climate adaptation in agriculture through a meta-synthesis of national climate change adaptation plans (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959).

Zimbabwe.narrative: CGIAR and its centers have addressed climate challenges in Zimbabwe through various initiatives focused on adaptation, mitigation, and water resource management.
Zimbabwe.adaptation:
• The Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT promoted climate change adaptation by ensuring a secure and diversified supply of quality seeds suitable for local conditions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/118375).
• CGIAR Climate Security team, SAPPC, and ACCORD developed a common vision for climate, peace, and security in Zimbabwe, highlighting strategies to address socio-political instability linked to climate impacts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172813).
• The World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF) assessed the current state of national Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) and developed country-specific roadmaps for monitoring and reporting on CSA (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99474).
• CGIAR researchers examined the nexus between climate change, migration, and social tensions in Zimbabwe, emphasizing the need for integrated policy responses (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/163721).
• CGIAR researchers explored how climate hazards exacerbate trauma and disrupt livelihoods in displacement settings, proposing strategies to build integrated responses that recognize displaced populations as active agents of resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/177405).
Zimbabwe.mitigation:
• CGIAR researchers assessed the net greenhouse gas (GHG) emission impacts of various agricultural interventions in Zimbabwe, highlighting practices like carbon sequestration and reduced burning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110575).
• The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security, in collaboration with the International Center for Tropical Agriculture, promoted climate-smart agriculture (CSA) in Zimbabwe, focusing on productivity, adaptation, and mitigation pillars (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97083).
Zimbabwe.water:
• CGIAR researchers analyzed how climate exacerbates root causes of conflict in Zimbabwe, identifying water scarcity and conflict in Bulawayo and Matabeleland North as key issues (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116267).
• CGIAR researchers highlighted how climate change impacts on water, land, and food systems undermine the availability of key natural resources, increasing competition over water access and use (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/141679).
• The Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT examined the impact of El Niño on rainfall and maize yields in the Limpopo River Basin, which includes parts of Zimbabwe (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/130905).

Congo.narrative:

South Africa.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and water security in South Africa through various research initiatives. Specifically:
South Africa.adaptation:
• IFPRI conducted a household survey in the Limpopo River Basin to provide policymakers with tools to adapt to global change, focusing on agricultural patterns, crop water use, and climate adaptation options (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IFCZ7R).
• CGIAR assessed the integration of peace and security into climate change policies in Southern Africa, highlighting the need for climate-responsive policies to address compounding risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173619).
• CGIAR synthesized existing evidence on climate change impacts in Southern Africa, emphasizing the need for regionwide actions to address water-energy-food issues (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114114).
• CGIAR examined the existing institutional frameworks for climate change to identify opportunities for incorporating the climate, peace, and security nexus in Southern Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175771).
• CGIAR developed climate-yield models tailored to supply chain-specific needs, creating insights into climate risks and opportunities for potato production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
• CGIAR compiled a detailed meta-dataset describing the context, management, and outcomes of livestock feed experiments in sub-Saharan Africa, including South Africa (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XY9WAX).
• CGIAR highlighted the need for sustainable agriculture based on integrated management of water, land, and ecosystems at the landscape scale (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34813).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, focusing on trade-offs and synergies related to decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR discussed the importance of intersectionality in building climate resilience, emphasizing the need for climate-smart interventions that take gender differences into account (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/131154).
South Africa.mitigation: • CGIAR emphasized the incorporation of climate change mitigation into agricultural development planning and investment strategies, focusing on sustainable agriculture practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34813).
South Africa.water:
• IFPRI conducted a household survey in the Limpopo River Basin to provide policymakers with tools to adapt to global change, focusing on water security and crop water use (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IFCZ7R).
• CGIAR synthesized existing evidence on climate change impacts in Southern Africa, emphasizing the need for regionwide actions to address water-energy-food issues (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114114).
• CGIAR examined the impacts of El Niño in the Limpopo River Basin, predicting reduced rainfall and higher temperatures (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/130905).

Togo.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation in Togo through capacity building and climate-smart agriculture. Specifically: 
- Bioversity International and the International Center for Tropical Agriculture analyzed climate vulnerabilities and adaptation potential in peanut, cashew, and soya bean value chains (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111345). 
- The CGIAR Research Programme on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security assessed the climate-smartness of the West Africa Agricultural Productivity Programme (WAAPP) in Togo (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).
Togo.adaptation:
• Bioversity International and the International Center for Tropical Agriculture provided a climate and vulnerability analysis of the Green Innovation Centres (GIC) target commodity value chains in Togo, identifying climate change-related vulnerabilities, hazards, and opportunities for adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111345).
• The CGIAR Research Programme on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security assessed the climate-smartness of the West Africa Agricultural Productivity Programme (WAAPP) in Togo, strengthening the capacity of stakeholders to mainstream and implement Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).
• Alliance Bioversity International and CIAT formulated and implemented climate-smart agriculture projects in Togo using integrated, participatory, and village-based approaches (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/144087).
• The CGIAR Research Programme on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security organized training sessions in Togo to reinforce knowledge on climate-smart villages, climate-smart agriculture approaches, and prioritization of climate-smart investments (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101491).
• AICCRA and FSRP programs collaborated to scale climate-smart agriculture in Togo through capacity building and promotion of evidence-based CSA technologies and innovations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179609).
• AICCRA supported the expansion of the Independent Global Stocktake Hub in Togo to promote inclusive climate action and policymaking (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179547).
• AICCRA conducted a regional needs assessment in Togo to mainstream climate-smart agriculture and climate information services into universities’ curricula (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179518).
Togo.mitigation: • AICCRA built the resource mobilization capacity of civil society organizations in Togo to design bankable climate finance concept notes, enabling them to leverage existing climate finance for locally relevant mitigation actions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).

Central African Republic.narrative: CGIAR centers conducted research in Central African Republic focusing on adaptation, mitigation, and water. Specifically:
Central African Republic.adaptation:
• CIMMYT mapped the vulnerability of 100 priority tree species in Central Africa to guide conservation and restoration efforts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, highlighting trade-offs and synergies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• AGNES conducted capacity-building trainings on the attribution of climate change and its impacts for African climate negotiators (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
• CGIAR analyzed the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR evaluated the relationship between adaptation-focused ODA, vulnerability, and GDP per capita (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382).
Central African Republic.mitigation: • CIFOR analyzed the narrative and promises of 'avoided deforestation' implementation in Central Africa, focusing on REDD+ projects (https://doi.org/10.1505/146554820829403469).
Central African Republic.water:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on the water cycle in the Congo Basin, including the Central African Republic (https://doi.org/10.3390/su12072936).
• CGIAR analyzed economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa, including water adaptation scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

Gabon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate research in Gabon through various initiatives. Specifically:
- CGIAR implemented capacity-building programs on climate-smart agriculture and climate information services (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514).
- CGIAR mapped vulnerability of tree species to guide conservation and restoration efforts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
- CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
- CGIAR conducted training on attribution of climate change and its impacts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
- CGIAR analyzed biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
- CGIAR assessed economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
- CGIAR analyzed narratives and promises of 'avoided deforestation' implementation (https://doi.org/10.1505/146554820829403469).
Gabon.adaptation:
• CGIAR built capacities of university lecturers in climate-smart agriculture technologies and climate information services (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514).

• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers, highlighting trade-offs and synergies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).

• CGIAR examined biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed two adaptation scenarios to climate change for Sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
Gabon.mitigation: • CGIAR analyzed narratives and promises of 'avoided deforestation' implementation in Central Africa (https://doi.org/10.1505/146554820829403469).
Gabon.water: • CGIAR assessed economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture, including water resources (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

Madagascar.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized climate adaptation strategies in Madagascar through various innovations. Specifically:
- Bioversity International implemented a seed resilience strategy to enhance farmers' access to crop diversity for climate change adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
- AfricaRice provided daily weather data for Ambohibary, Madagascar, from 2019 to 2020 (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/GWCGGB).
Madagascar.adaptation:
• Bioversity International implemented a seed resilience strategy to enhance farmers' access to crop diversity for climate change adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
• CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on maize production and food security, highlighting the need for adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR conducted a rapid climate risk assessment to identify hotspots of vulnerability to extreme rainfall and drought hazards in Madagascar (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109192).
• CGIAR analyzed climate change vulnerability in the agricultural sector of the Union of the Comoros, which is situated between Mozambique and Madagascar, focusing on managing climate risks for small-scale farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78667).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, including Madagascar, to understand trade-offs and synergies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR profiled available climate change-related datasets and their accessibility in Madagascar, assessing the incorporation of climate change adaptation strategies in national development plans (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33403).
• CGIAR analyzed the impact of climate change on African economies, including Madagascar, and opportunities for agrifood system transformation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/155080).
• CGIAR summarized East African agriculture and climate change, focusing on Madagascar, to analyze agricultural policies and sustainability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154082).
Madagascar.water:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa, including Madagascar, to build resilient communities (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR examined climate change impacts on maize production and food security, including water management strategies (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Gambia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in Gambia through capacity building and adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed climate-smart agriculture curricula (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514).
- CGIAR assessed adaptation measures using protection motivation theory (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli7010013).
Gambia.adaptation:
• CGIAR built capacities in climate-smart agriculture and climate information services through university curricula (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514).
• CGIAR conducted a regional needs assessment for mainstreaming climate-smart agriculture and climate information services into university curricula (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179518).
• CGIAR documented climate-smart agriculture options for adaptation planning in Gambia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111156).
• CGIAR provided a snapshot of baseline for investing in climate-smart agriculture at scale (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98275).
• CGIAR utilized data for developing successful projects for the Green Climate Fund (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).
• CGIAR highlighted women's leadership in climate change adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/176557).
• CGIAR reviewed trade-offs and synergies of climate change adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR assessed heat stress conditions for dairy cattle production (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2).
• CGIAR described farmers’ adoption of climate change adaptation measures using protection motivation theory (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli7010013).
Gambia.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched soil carbon sequestration and crop production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514).
• CGIAR identified technologies and practices for reducing GHGs emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111156).
Gambia.water: • CGIAR collected household survey data on integrated land and water management for adaptation to climate variability and change (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

Tajikistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient development in Tajikistan through targeted research and policy support. Specifically: 
- IFPRI assessed nutrition-sensitive value chains in Tajikistan's agriculture and water sectors (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BMKEMG). 
- IFPRI conducted interim surveys to evaluate ongoing activities in Tajikistan's Zone of Influence (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DHRV46).
Tajikistan.adaptation:
• IFPRI mapped institutions and stakeholders to strengthen climate change action coordination in Tajikistan (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138814).
• IFPRI proposed a framework to integrate agri-food systems with climate change policies in Tajikistan (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138815).
• IFPRI analyzed climate change effects on food security in Tajikistan using the IMPACT model (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/141633).
• IFPRI conducted the Tajikistan Rural Household Survey to study rural livelihoods and resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/YPTNYW).
• IFPRI and the Committee for Environmental Protection developed an NDC Implementation Plan for Tajikistan (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136837).
• IFPRI and the Tajik Academy of Agricultural Sciences collected data on rural household resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/YPTNYW).
Tajikistan.mitigation: • IFPRI contributed to Tajikistan's NDCs, focusing on sustainable development and climate change mitigation (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136837).

Equatorial Guinea.narrative: CGIAR centers conducted research on climate change impacts and adaptation strategies in Equatorial Guinea, focusing on vulnerability mapping, climate-smart agriculture, and maize production under climate change.
Equatorial Guinea.adaptation:
• Ceccarelli et al. mapped the vulnerability of 100 priority tree species in Central Africa, including Equatorial Guinea, to guide conservation and restoration efforts under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
• Akinyi et al. reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, highlighting trade-offs and synergies in decision-making (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• Tesfaye Fantaye et al. examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa, including potential adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• Zougmoré et al. reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies in West Africa, with implications for climate-smart agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
Equatorial Guinea.water: • Calzadilla et al. analyzed economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa, including adaptation scenarios that involve water resources (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

France.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and mitigation in France through policy frameworks and research initiatives. Specifically: 
- The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security highlighted progress in climate-smart agriculture and adaptation measures in Kenya and Uganda ahead of COP21 in Paris (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68858). 
- The Montpellier Statement was endorsed by over 600 researchers and 150 stakeholders during the 3rd Global Science Conference on Climate-Smart Agriculture in Montpellier, France, emphasizing the integration of food security, adaptation, and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67193).
France.adaptation:
• The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security discussed climate-smart agriculture and adaptation measures in East Africa, including improving productivity of goats and sheep under changing climatic conditions, ahead of COP21 in Paris (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68858).
• The Montpellier Statement, endorsed during the 3rd Global Science Conference on Climate-Smart Agriculture in Montpellier, France, emphasized the integration of food security, adaptation, and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67193).
• The World Bank, in collaboration with CGIAR, highlighted the importance of adapting to climate change and reducing greenhouse gas emissions as part of the Paris Agreement defined at COP21 in France (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259).
• The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security also contributed to the development of climate-smart agriculture investment plans (CSAIPs) to address climate change risks to agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259).

Côte D'ivoire.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation in Côte D'Ivoire through various research initiatives. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed the economic case for climate action in cocoa production (Source_1).
- CGIAR predicted future climatic suitability for cocoa farming (Source_2).
- CGIAR developed water management options for malaria and climate change mitigation in rice production (Source_3).
Côte D'ivoire.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the economic case for climate action in cocoa production, highlighting income losses from inaction and the need for adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100126).
• CGIAR predicted future climatic suitability for cocoa farming in Côte D'Ivoire using species distribution modeling (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27779).
• CGIAR identified the projected impact of climate change on cocoa value chains and strategies to improve resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79868).
• CGIAR analyzed climate change impacts on rice yields and evaluated management practices to reduce yield losses (https://doi.org/10.1002/agj2.70113).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of urbanization on land use dynamics and local climate, including extreme weather events and urban heat island phenomena (https://doi.org/10.3934/geosci.2025010).
Côte D'ivoire.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched water management options for malaria and climate change mitigation in intensified rice production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/122191).
• CGIAR evaluated management practices to mitigate yield losses and increase rice productivity under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1002/agj2.70113).
Côte D'ivoire.water: • CGIAR developed water management approaches to reduce malaria transmission potential, greenhouse gas emissions, and water use while increasing rice yield (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/122191).

Belize.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies in Belize through policy development and technical support. Specifically:
- The International Center for Tropical Agriculture and the World Bank developed a framework for identifying and prioritizing Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices in Belize (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100495).
- The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security led the technical component in the formulation of the CSA regional strategy for the Central American Integration System (SICA) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121429).
Belize.adaptation:
• The International Center for Tropical Agriculture and the World Bank developed a framework for identifying and prioritizing Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices in Belize (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100495).
• The CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security led the technical component in the formulation of the CSA regional strategy for the Central American Integration System (SICA) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121429).

Jamaica.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and food security in Jamaica through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically: 
- CIAT assessed climate change impacts on cocoa and tomato production (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70144). 
- CGIAR implemented climate-smart agriculture practices and extension services (Sources_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/117966, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/118036, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/118038, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/118039). 
- CGIAR conducted a needs assessment for greenhouse gas emissions reporting (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/176340).
Jamaica.adaptation:
• CIAT assessed the impact of climate change on cocoa and tomato production, highlighting suitability changes and policy recommendations (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70144).
• CGIAR developed a capacity-building program to improve stakeholder resilience and adaptation to climate change, including water-balance models and climate-smart agriculture practices (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/117966).
• CGIAR created Climate-Smart Agriculture extension services manuals for Essex Valley, Parnassus, and Amity Hall, incorporating local farmer experiences and expert guidance (Sources_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/118036, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/118038, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/118039).
• CGIAR reviewed governance structures for climate services, focusing on agro-meteorological services for smallholder farmers (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96255).
• CGIAR analyzed socio-economic impacts of climate change on food supply chains and proposed adaptation policies (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/45989).
• CGIAR promoted climate-smart agriculture policies to secure Jamaica's food supply (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99579).
Jamaica.mitigation: • CGIAR conducted a needs assessment for agriculture and LULUCF inventory systems to improve greenhouse gas emissions reporting (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/176340).
Jamaica.water: • CGIAR integrated water-balance models and irrigated systems to address crop water demand under climate change (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/117966).

Korea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and food security in Korea through strategic planning and adaptation measures. Specifically: 
- CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security supported APEC economies to develop a multi-year plan on climate change and food security (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197). 
- CGIAR assessed the prospects of mangrove species for carbon capture expansion in Korean coastal areas (https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-11394).

Austria.narrative:
CGIAR centers contributed to climate research in Austria through various initiatives. Specifically: 
- ILRI and WorldFish, alongside partners, proposed food systems as a key topic for the Sharm el-Sheikh mitigation ambition and implementation work programme (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087). 
- Manners, Rhys, and colleagues analyzed the impact of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974). 
- Vinceti, Barbara, and colleagues surveyed forest owners and managers on managing forest genetic resources to adapt to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6). 
- Schoniger, F., and colleagues studied the impact of climate change on future electricity generation and demand patterns in Europe (https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862). 
- Ignaciuk A and Mason-D'Croz modeled adaptation measures in agriculture to mitigate climate change effects (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Austria.adaptation:
• Manners, Rhys, and colleagues analyzed the heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe, emphasizing the need for breeding and research planning strategies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
• Vinceti, Barbara, and colleagues surveyed forest owners and managers on managing forest genetic resources to strengthen the resilience of forest ecosystems to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• Schoniger, F., and colleagues assessed the impact of climate change on future electricity generation and demand patterns in Europe, focusing on hydropower and extreme weather events (https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862).
• Ignaciuk A and Mason-D'Croz modeled adaptation measures in agriculture, including research and development for new crop varieties and changes in irrigation technology (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Austria.mitigation: • ILRI and WorldFish, alongside partners, proposed food systems as a key topic for the Sharm el-Sheikh mitigation ambition and implementation work programme (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087).
Austria.water: • Schoniger, F., and colleagues studied the impact of climate change on hydropower generation and hydro run-of-river (RoR) systems in Europe (https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862).

United Arab Emirates.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and mitigation in the United Arab Emirates through scientific expertise and policy contributions. Specifically: 
- CGIAR contributed to the development of the Belém Adaptation Indicators under the UAE–Belém work programme (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179973). 
- CGIAR emphasized the importance of integrating agrifood systems into just transition planning (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181090).
United Arab Emirates.adaptation:
• CGIAR played a direct role in shaping the Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA) outcome at COP30, contributing scientific expertise to the development of the Belém Adaptation Indicators under the United Arab Emirates–Belém work programme (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179973).
• CGIAR provided an overview of the Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA), highlighting its establishment in 2015 and the progress in operationalising and measuring the GGA, including the UAE-Belem work programme on indicators (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175062).
• CGIAR organized a South-South Cooperation event to facilitate exchange and learnings on scaling climate-smart agricultural index-based insurances (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472).
• CGIAR, alongside partners, completed a submission to the third dialogue of the United Arab Emirates Just Transition Work Programme, emphasizing the critical importance of integrating agrifood systems into just transition planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181090).
• CGIAR highlighted a researcher’s perspective on how to measure adaptation effectiveness, showcasing practical application of these principles in the development of tools for tracking adaptation in the agriculture sectors in Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138322).
• CGIAR and the Eastern Africa Farmers Federation submitted a joint response to Decision 2/CMA.5, Paragraph 41 regarding the United Arab Emirates Belem Work Programme on indicators, focusing on adaptation practices and strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169062).
United Arab Emirates.mitigation: • CGIAR, alongside the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), submitted views on opportunities, best practices, actionable solutions, challenges, and barriers relevant to the second dialogue under the United Arab Emirates Just Transition Work Programme, focusing on the transition to a greener economy (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169068).

Mauritius.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation strategies and water management in Mauritius through systematic reviews and integrated approaches. Specifically:
- CGIAR conducted a systematic review on trade-offs and synergies of climate adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa (Source 1).
- CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa (Source 2).
Mauritius.adaptation:
• CGIAR conducted a systematic review on trade-offs and synergies of climate adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, highlighting the complexity of decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa, emphasizing the need for integrated and transformative systems approaches (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa, using adapted improved maize varieties and bioeconomic models (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed two adaptation scenarios to climate change for Sub-Saharan Africa, focusing on increasing irrigated area and crop yields (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
Mauritius.water:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts on water resources and the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa, highlighting adaptation opportunities and challenges (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical impacts of climate change on maize production and food security, including water management strategies (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed adaptation scenarios involving water resources, such as doubling the irrigated area and increasing crop yields (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

CGIAR prioritized adaptation strategies in Greece through research on protein-rich crops and agricultural resilience. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability and proposed breeding strategies to improve resilience (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
- CGIAR modeled adaptation measures, including new crop varieties and irrigation technology, to mitigate negative effects of climate change (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Mayotte.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation strategies in Mayotte through systematic reviews and bioeconomic modeling. Specifically:
- CGIAR conducted a systematic review on trade-offs and synergies of climate adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
- CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
- CGIAR analyzed economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture using adaptation scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
Mayotte.adaptation:

Turkmenistan.narrative: CGIAR conducted research on climate vulnerabilities and adaptation strategies in Turkmenistan, focusing on water resources, agricultural productivity, and livestock systems.
Turkmenistan.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate adaptation and mitigation strategies, including adaptive capacities and climate resilience in Central Asia's water, agriculture, and energy sectors (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR identified adaptation strategies for rangeland systems under climate change, highlighting the need for pastoral system mitigation and adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
• CGIAR advocated for innovative research pathways to scrutinize smallholder adaptation strategies and examine the nexus between climate change, water scarcity, and mobility in rural Central Asia (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).
Turkmenistan.mitigation: • CGIAR implied GHG mitigation through the context of achieving carbon neutrality by the middle of the century (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Turkmenistan.water:
• CGIAR examined climate vulnerabilities of Central Asia's water sectors, including projections of river discharge under RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 scenarios (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR analyzed water scarcity and its impact on irrigated agriculture, driven by climate change and increasing water demand (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).

CGIAR prioritized Mitigation in Nauru through a near-term actionable mitigation agenda. Specifically:

Georgia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate vulnerabilities and adaptive capacities in Central Asia, including Georgia, through sectoral assessments. Specifically: 
- CGIAR examined climate vulnerabilities of water, agriculture, and energy sectors (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220). 
- CGIAR assessed adaptive capacities and economic burdens of climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Georgia.adaptation: • CGIAR examined climate adaptation and mitigation measures, focusing on adaptive capacities and climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Georgia.mitigation: • CGIAR researched GHG mitigation and carbon neutrality goals (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Georgia.water: • CGIAR assessed climate vulnerabilities of water resources, including river discharge projections under RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 scenarios (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).

general / cross-cutting.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized cross-cutting research in Africa, India, Somalia, Burkina Faso, and Papua New Guinea through various innovations. Specifically: 
- IFPRI developed financial feasibility maps for solar irrigation under climate change scenarios (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/J0Q9TO). 
- IFPRI assessed the impact of an integrated agriculture-nutrition intervention in Burkina Faso (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ). 
- IFPRI and ECF scoped a living lab for locally led climate adaptation in Nigeria (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180016). 
- IFPRI evaluated Kenya’s climate adaptation and mitigation frameworks for innovation uptake (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181177). 
- IFPRI explored the nexus between climate change, mobility, and conflict in Somalia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173200). 
- IFPRI reviewed climate-resilient water infrastructures in India (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180825). 
- IFPRI developed a bottom-up framework for assessing greenhouse gas emissions in food systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179911). 
- IFPRI conducted a rural household survey in Papua New Guinea (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BYZMZ6).
general / cross-cutting.adaptation:
• IFPRI synthesized evidence from 2,916 agricultural studies across Africa to evaluate climate-resilient agronomy practices (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).
• IFPRI implemented an integrated agriculture-nutrition intervention in Burkina Faso to enhance nutritional outcomes (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ).
• IFPRI and ECF scoped a living lab for locally led climate adaptation in Nigeria (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180016).
• IFPRI assessed Kenya’s climate adaptation frameworks for innovation uptake (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181177).
• IFPRI explored the interplay between climate change, mobility, and conflict in Somalia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173200).
• IFPRI reviewed climate-resilient water infrastructures in India (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180825).
• IFPRI conducted a rural household survey in Papua New Guinea to analyze resilient local food systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BYZMZ6).
general / cross-cutting.mitigation:
• IFPRI developed a bottom-up framework for assessing greenhouse gas emissions and mitigation potential in food systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179911).
• IFPRI assessed Kenya’s climate mitigation frameworks for innovation uptake (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181177).
general / cross-cutting.water:
• IFPRI developed financial feasibility maps for solar irrigation under climate change scenarios in Africa (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/J0Q9TO).
• IFPRI implemented an integrated agriculture-nutrition intervention in Burkina Faso, explicitly mentioning water (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ).
• IFPRI reviewed climate-resilient water infrastructures in India (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180825).

rice.narrative: CGIAR centers prioritized rice research across adaptation, mitigation, and water management through various innovations. Specifically:
rice.adaptation:
• IFPRI analyzed climate impact on rice yields under different emissions scenarios and climate models (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
• IFPRI promoted drought- and flood-tolerant rice varieties and climate-smart agriculture practices in Mali (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179427).
• IFPRI explored future rice market segments and trait priorities to enhance climate resilience in Southeast Asia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174900).
• IFPRI scaled digital advisory tools like RiceAdvice to support climate-smart rice production in Mali (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179425).
• IFPRI developed climate-related risk maps and adaptation plans for rice production in Vietnam’s Mekong River Delta (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/90253).
• IFPRI examined climate-induced quality changes in temperate-grown rice and adaptive strategies (https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202505.2196.v1).
rice.mitigation:
• IFPRI explored policies for low-emission rice production practices in Southeast Asia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/163419).
• IFPRI analyzed rice cultivation actions in Nationally Determined Contributions, highlighting mitigation potential (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116169).
• IFPRI proposed an investment plan for low-emission rice production using the AWD technique in Vietnam (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101315).
rice.water:
• IFPRI identified water management as a key strategy for both mitigation and adaptation in rice production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116169).
• IFPRI discussed water-efficient practices and precision agriculture for temperate rice production (https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202505.2196.v1).
• IFPRI highlighted the System of Rice Intensification (SRI) and water management for climate-smart agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/77040).

livestock (general/beef).narrative: CGIAR centers have contributed to livestock (general/beef) research across adaptation, mitigation, and water categories through various innovations and assessments.
livestock (general/beef).adaptation:
• ILRI and CGIAR developed the Animal Ontology for Livestock Trials (AOM) v2 to standardize livestock feed trial data, supporting resilience in diverse agro-ecological systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/75E7HV).
• ILRI, CGIAR, and others outlined strategies for livestock adaptation, including managing herd composition, grassland restoration, and silvopastoralism to survive increasing climate change hazards (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172688).
• CIAT developed improved forages to enhance livestock productivity and climate change adaptation, focusing on drought tolerance and better feed supplies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173226).
• CGIAR assessed top adaptation actions for African livestock systems, including sustainable rangeland management and reducing methane emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108139).
• ILRI proposed a landscape approach for sustainable livestock systems, improving grazing conditions in Colombia's Orinoquia region (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110369).
livestock (general/beef).mitigation:
• ILRI and CGIAR outlined mitigation strategies for the livestock sector, including low-emission livestock types, silvopastoralism, and manure management to meet net zero targets by 2050 (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172688).
• ILRI and CGIAR launched the Livestock and Climate Solutions Hub for LMICs to transition to sustainable and low-emission livestock systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181032).
• CGIAR analyzed livestock management ambition in nationally determined contributions, highlighting priorities like manure management and feed management for mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115885).
• CGIAR examined policy coherence for livestock sector mitigation in Kenya, Ethiopia, and Uganda, identifying gaps and opportunities for adaptation-mitigation synergies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101262).
livestock (general/beef).water: • ILRI and CGIAR analyzed how climate exacerbates pressure on land, water, and pasture in Sudan, affecting livestock production and food security (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128061).

agroforestry.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized agroforestry for climate resilience and carbon mitigation through various innovations. Specifically: 
- IFPRI examined policy options for agroforestry uptake (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SYLMLP). 
- ICRAF explored agroforestry's role in climate adaptation and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98404).
agroforestry.adaptation:
• IFPRI examined policy options for agroforestry uptake (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SYLMLP).
• ICRAF explored agroforestry's role in climate adaptation and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98404).
• ICRAF highlighted agroforestry as a climate adaptation strategy for smallholder farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/155481).
• ICRAF documented agroforestry's potential to enhance resilience in West and Central Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179555).
• ICRAF assessed agroforestry's financial and environmental benefits in Myanmar (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114390).
• ICRAF presented agroforestry as a nature-based approach to reverse environmental degradation in East Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/176734).
agroforestry.mitigation:

• ICRAF highlighted agroforestry's contribution to carbon sequestration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/155481).
• ICRAF documented mitigation through forest restoration and agroforestry in West and Central Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179555).
• ICRAF assessed agroforestry's carbon sequestration potential in Myanmar (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114390).
• ICRAF evaluated agricultural carbon projects for GHG emissions reduction (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110876).

maize.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized maize adaptation strategies across multiple regions through various innovations. Specifically:
- CIMMYT developed climate services to reduce agricultural production risks in South Asia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/89961).
- IITA and SARI explored sustainable intensification of cereal-based farming systems in northern Ghana (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/8UFV7X).
maize.adaptation:
• CIMMYT evaluated the impact of climate change on maize yields in Guatemala, projecting a 14% reduction by 2050, and recommended adaptation policies for policymakers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146593).
• IITA and SARI assessed the impact of climate change on maize productivity in Honduras, finding a 12% yield reduction due to climate change (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215).
• CIMMYT supported the development of agricultural climate services in South Asia to increase the resilience of smallholder farming communities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/89961).
• CIMMYT promoted climate-smart agriculture practices in Bihar, India, to increase the resilience and adaptive potential of agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96141).
• CIMMYT collected data from climate and soil sensors in Guatemala to improve the productivity and sustainability of maize systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DGLBH7).
• IFPRI and MIT simulated the impact of weather events on maize yields in Ethiopia to develop adaptive strategies for sustainable agriculture (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).
• CIMMYT investigated the profitability of climate-smart agriculture practices, such as crop rotation and intercropping of maize with soybean, in rural Tanzania (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108270).
• IITA and SARI explored sustainable intensification of cereal-based farming systems in northern Ghana, including the use of Zai farming technology and fertilizer micro-dosing (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/J6BHT8).
maize.water:
• IITA and SARI collected data on soil moisture and light interception in maize-based cropping systems in northern Ghana (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/8UFV7X).
• ICRAF and SUA developed fertilizer application rates for maize in the semi-arid zone of Tanzania to sustain soil fertility (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/6YPPQO).

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized wheat research through various innovations in adaptation and mitigation. Specifically:
- IFPRI assessed the effects of COVID-19 lockdowns on wheat farmers' crop losses and food security (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PAWM4U).
- Bioversity International and CIAT analyzed climate change-related vulnerabilities and adaptation strategies for wheat value chains (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111337).
wheat.adaptation:
• IFPRI assessed the effects of COVID-19 lockdowns on wheat farmers' crop losses, harvesting practices, and food security (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PAWM4U).
• IFPRI, Ghent University, and University of Manchester developed smartphone-based near-surface remote sensing to monitor wheat growth and production risks (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DBAFZY).
• Bioversity International and CIAT analyzed climate change-related vulnerabilities and adaptation strategies for wheat value chains (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111337).
• Bioversity International and CIAT explored policy issues and recommendations for enhancing access to agricultural genetic resources for climate-change adaptation in East Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116203).
• Ambaw, Tadesse, and Recha implemented climate-smart agriculture practices to enhance farmer resilience and crop productivity in Ethiopia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106308).
• van Etten et al. tested crowdsourced citizen science to improve variety recommendations for wheat in India (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116).
wheat.mitigation: • Tadesse et al. investigated the effects of climate-smart agriculture on soil fertility, crop yield, and soil carbon sequestration in Southern Ethiopia (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515).

roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized adaptation strategies for roots and tubers through various innovations. Specifically:
- HarvestPlus and the International Potato Center (CIP) conducted the Reaching End Users (REU) project to distribute biofortified orange-fleshed sweet potato (OFSP) in Mozambique and Uganda (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RN40SP).
- The International Potato Center developed climate-smart varieties of potatoes and sweetpotatoes that tolerate heat, drought, or saline conditions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106939).
roots and tubers.adaptation:
• HarvestPlus and the International Potato Center (CIP) implemented the Reaching End Users (REU) project to promote the adoption of biofortified orange-fleshed sweet potato (OFSP) in Mozambique and Uganda (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RN40SP).
• The International Potato Center developed climate-smart varieties of potatoes and sweetpotatoes that tolerate heat, drought, or saline conditions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106939).
• CIAT and Vietnamese partners conducted a study on the adoption of improved cassava varieties and assessed impacts on farm productivity and income in Vietnam (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4HJJXZ).
• PepsiCo, in collaboration with CGIAR-affiliated researchers, developed climate-yield models and the Climate Resilience Platform to enhance supply-chain resilience in potato production across 12 countries (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
• CGIAR researchers assessed climate change risks and opportunities in the potato sector in Kenya, highlighting the potential of climate-smart agriculture practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103233).
• CGIAR researchers conducted a climate risk assessment in Uganda, emphasizing the need for climate-smart agriculture practices to reduce economic losses and build resilience in the potato sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107721).
• CIAT systematically evaluated cassava germplasm accessions for adaptation to different soils and climates, resistance to pests and diseases, and quality traits (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KM8CQG).
roots and tubers.water: • CIAT and partners conducted suitability mapping for cassava in the Mekong Delta, Cambodia, considering irrigation and other management practices to identify optimal growing locations and seasons (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173187).

legumes.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized legume research across adaptation and mitigation themes. Specifically: 
- Bioversity and CIAT developed a guide for agronomic management of bush beans (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116328). 
- CIAT assessed climate risk in Kenyan common beans (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107723).
legumes.adaptation:
• Bioversity and CIAT developed a guide for agronomic management of bush beans to support smallholder farmers in Guatemala (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116328).
• CIAT assessed climate risk in Kenyan common beans, highlighting declining production and food security challenges (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107723).
• CIAT and partners reported on potato-legume intercropping to enhance climate resilience and adaptive capacity in Kenya (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108045).
• CIAT and World Bank identified climate-smart agriculture practices to build resilience in Uganda's soybean sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103239).
• CIAT and partners analyzed climate change risks and opportunities for common beans in Tanzania (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103235).
• CIAT and partners spatially classified high-impact spots of climate change for drybeans production in Central America (https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/heqqih).
• HarvestPlus and IFPRI assessed adoption and diffusion rates of high iron beans in Rwanda (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/G2MFQP).
• Bioversity and CIAT analyzed climate change-related vulnerabilities and adaptation priorities for legume value chains in Togo (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111345).
• CIAT identified climate-smart agriculture practices to build resilience in Kenya's green gram sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103232).
• Bioversity and partners supported crop variety management for climate adaptation through farmer participation in on-farm research (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).
legumes.mitigation:
• Bioversity and CIAT developed a guide for agronomic management of bush beans, which also contributes to mitigation efforts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116328).
• CIAT and World Bank identified climate-smart agriculture practices that contribute to climate change mitigation in Uganda's soybean sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103239).
• CIAT identified climate-smart agriculture practices that contribute to climate change mitigation in Kenya's green gram sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103232).

coffee and cocoa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in coffee and cocoa production through targeted adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on cocoa production in West and Central Africa (Source_1). 
- CGIAR developed adaptation frameworks for coffee production in Nicaragua (Source_2).
coffee and cocoa.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed solar-powered groundwater irrigation solutions for climate-resilient cocoa farming in Ghana (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agwat.2025.110118).
• CGIAR quantified the impact of progressive climate change on coffee production in Nicaragua and developed an adaptation framework across time and space (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/TSUPE1).
• CGIAR identified climate change adaptation strategies for coffee production in Central America, highlighting the need for crop-/site- and actor-specific approaches (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93348).
• CGIAR analyzed climate change impacts on coffee production in Honduras, emphasizing the vulnerability of Arabica coffee to higher temperatures and variable precipitation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97530).
• CGIAR evaluated potential cocoa productivity responses to climate change by mid-century, projecting yield increases and suitable area expansions in West and Central Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2025.110393).
• CGIAR assessed the vulnerability of cocoa farms in Côte d’Ivoire to climate-related risks and the need for resilience to extreme events and gradual changes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103790).
• CGIAR mapped the agro-ecological distribution suitable for coffee cultivation in Central America and indicated the impact gradient of climate change on suitable zones (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9QUGUR).
coffee and cocoa.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched climate-smart agriculture (CSA) initiatives to reduce/remove greenhouse gases (GHGs) in coffee production in Uganda (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101331).
• CGIAR explored the integration of mitigation and adaptation strategies in Climate Smart Cocoa (CSC) to improve livelihoods and productivity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103770).
• CGIAR highlighted the need for sustainably increasing productivity, enhancing resilience, and reducing/removing greenhouse gases (GHGs) in Climate Smart Coffee (CSC) in El Salvador (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103773).

fisheries and aquaculture.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized fisheries and aquaculture through various adaptations and assessments. Specifically:
- IFPRI assessed coping strategies in flood-affected rural areas (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UJA9N5).
- IFPRI analyzed climate change impacts on coastal fisheries in Papua New Guinea (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136871).
fisheries and aquaculture.adaptation:
• IFPRI assessed coping strategies in flood-affected rural areas, focusing on recovery from shock and food security (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UJA9N5).
• IFPRI analyzed climate change impacts on coastal fisheries, including coral bleaching and negative impacts on health (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136871).
• WorldFish assessed on-farm performance of tilapia aquaculture systems in Egypt (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VSQWEE).
• IFPRI developed gender integration strategies to improve the resilience of selected agriculture value chains, prioritizing fish (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/U9FLMU).

dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient strategies for dryland cereals (sorghum/millets) through various innovations. Specifically:
- ICRISAT evaluated crop simulation models to improve sorghum productivity (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J).
- CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security promoted weather-resilient technologies for pearl millet (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109724).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).adaptation:
• ICRISAT evaluated crop simulation models using different fertility sources and climate model outputs to improve sorghum productivity in Mali (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J).
• CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security promoted weather-resilient technologies for pearl millet in Mathura, India (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109724).
• Acre Africa and Shalem Investments, enabled by CGIAR innovations, provided credit for drought-resistant seeds and climate-smart advisory services in Kenya (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174497).
• ICRISAT assessed the impacts of weather and climate information services on technical efficiency and sorghum productivity in Ghana (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116354).
• CGIAR implemented a project to enhance access to genetic resources for climate change adaptation in Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111207).
• ICRISAT modeled sorghum yield response to climate change in Ethiopia, highlighting the need for climate-resilient strategies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jafr.2025.102143).
• ICRISAT investigated climate risk adaptation strategies for pearl millet in Nigeria and Senegal (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137574).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).water:
• ICRISAT evaluated the impacts of weather and climate information services on technical efficiency and sorghum productivity in Ghana (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116354).
• ICRISAT assessed seasonal and intra-seasonal skill at Kaffrine station in Senegal, focusing on millet crop yields (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33397).

bananas.narrative:
Bioversity International prioritized drought tolerance and water efficiency in banana production through phenotyping and genetic analysis. Specifically:
- Bioversity International investigated drought-tolerant cultivars and stress tolerance mechanisms (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68445).
- Bioversity International used hydroponics and transpiration phenotyping to select water-efficient banana genotypes (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5).
bananas.adaptation:
• Bioversity International evaluated banana cultivars under osmotic stress to assess drought tolerance (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q0ESWS).
• Bioversity International and CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security empowered the export banana sector in climate-smart decision-making (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121261).
• Zheng et al. conducted transcriptomic analysis to identify differentially expressed genes in resistant and susceptible banana corms against Fusarium oxysporum (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG).
bananas.water:
• Bioversity International phenotyped banana plants to reduce dependency on irrigation water and identify drought-tolerant cultivars (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68445).
• Wesemael et al. used growth and transpiration phenotyping to select water-efficient banana genotypes (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5).
• Yonow et al. assessed climate suitability and disease pressure of black Sigatoka in bananas, considering strategic irrigation (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601).

fruits and vegetables.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized fruits and vegetables research across various themes. Specifically: 
- IFPRI conducted a vegetable value chain survey in Ethiopia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q55PL6).

fruits and vegetables.adaptation:

• Kafle et al. evaluated temperature-based suitability for mango across elevation gradients in Nepal, highlighting future fruit zoning and climate adaptation planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178854).
• Obossou et al. reviewed climate change adaptation in vegetable farming systems in Africa, focusing on water-related hazards and technological responses (https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7).

forages and pastures.narrative:
The International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) prioritized forages and pastures adaptation through germplasm evaluation and agronomic assessments. Specifically:
- CIAT developed a manual for agronomic evaluation of tropical pastures to standardize techniques and assess germplasm adaptation across ecosystems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS).
- CIAT conducted RIEPT trials to evaluate pasture germplasm adaptation potential under different grazing management systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
- CIAT selected Brachiaria accessions with superior agronomic traits and assessed climate and soil effects on yield (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NGL4OG).
- CIAT studied genotype-environment interactions in Desmodium ovalifolium to understand soil fertility and climate effects on tannin contents (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/COQEK8).
- CIAT characterized tropical forages germplasm for adaptation to the Quilichao environment, including yield potential and disease resistance (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VGOIIQ).
- CIAT and CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security evaluated land suitability and climate change impacts on alfalfa production in Ethiopia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).
- CIAT and CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security facilitated knowledge transfer and international collaboration for pasture and agronomy systems research (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096).

general cereals.narrative:
CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security prioritized sustainable cereal production in India through innovative water management and climate adaptation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security re-designed irrigated intensive cereal systems for agricultural sustainability in North-West India (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017). 
- CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security evaluated the impact of climate change on cereal production using econometric techniques (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403).
general cereals.adaptation: • CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security examined the long-term and short-term effects of climatic and non-climatic variables on cereal production in India, emphasizing the need for proactive measures to reduce adverse impacts of climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403).
general cereals.water: • CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security re-designed irrigated intensive cereal systems through bundling precision agronomic innovations for transitioning towards agricultural sustainability in North-West India (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017).
